# ARC-QUIC-RFC9001-0008 - QUIC RFC 9001 Non-Retained Stateless Reset Suppression Architecture

## Purpose

Define the suppressive post-stop matrix cell that rejects stateless-reset response emission when a later packet does not resolve to a retained route or lacks a remembered token, while keeping the positive retained-route floor separate.

## Requirements Satisfied

- REQ-QUIC-RFC9001-S6P6-0009

## Design Summary

The retained-route lookup helper remains exact: later packets only qualify for stateless-reset emission when they resolve to a retained route tied to a remembered token for the same remote address and port. If the lookup fails, or if the route is present but the token snapshot is absent, the endpoint helper returns a token-unavailable disposition before loop-prevention, rate-budget, or formatting logic can claim success. Connected endpoint and listener hosts continue to delegate through the same helper so the suppressive branch stays aligned across direct emission and receive-loop forwarding.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicConnectionEndpointHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicListenerHost.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicStatelessReset.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9001/REQ-QUIC-RFC9001-S6P6-0009.cs
- benchmarks/QuicStatelessResetBenchmarks.cs

## Data and State Considerations

The endpoint already preserves route bindings, token snapshots, and path identity after AEAD-limit terminal discard. This slice keeps the lookup boundary exact by treating missing retained-route state or missing remembered-token state as TokenUnavailable rather than as a new response mode. That lets the positive retained-route floor and the known-reset loop-suppression cell remain separate while the suppressive topoff is traced explicitly.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- Unknown routes must suppress emission.
- Routes without a remembered token must suppress emission.
- The retained-route positive floor remains governed by the same endpoint helper and separate requirement slice.
- Short-header and long-header lookup failures both remain suppressive at the same helper boundary.
- The connected endpoint host and listener host continue to use the shared helper; no host-local token comparison is introduced.

## Alternatives Considered

- Let non-retained packets proceed to formatting and emit a reset anyway. Rejected because it would overstate support and hide missing retained-route state.
- Merge the route-miss and missing-token branches into the known-reset suppression slice. Rejected because the suppressive topoff is a distinct helper outcome and should remain traceable on its own.
- Introduce a separate ambiguity-detection model before the runtime expresses one. Rejected because the current route model is exact and the missing work is a suppressive topoff, not an ambiguity engine.

## Risks

- If the no-retained-route and no-token branches are conflated with the retained-route floor, the endpoint can overclaim response eligibility.
- If the host shells reconstruct their own token or route-miss policy, the suppressive cell can drift away from the shared endpoint helper.
- If route misses and token misses are not kept separate from the known-reset loop branch, later matrix slices can lose their boundary.

## Open Questions

- If a future ambiguity state is added to route lookup, it should receive a separate requirement and proof plan instead of widening this suppressive topoff.
